narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 61% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong commitments like planning to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family, along with being a white male, which aligns closely with the majority of applicants. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 sits below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 25 is notably lower than the usual 36.7, and your school deciles for Year 1 and Year 3 grades both at 2—meaning you ranked in the bottom 20% of your peers those years—stand out as weaker spots compared to most students who place around the middle or higher. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your LSAT score, also nudging the outlook toward failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile in third place for impact. Your family income and undergraduate GPA came next in influence, both contributing in the direction of higher failure risk, while your racial background, full-time status, and gender had the least sway, still leaning slightly toward that unfavorable prediction. Overall, these academic performance indicators from your early years and entrance exams carried the heaviest weight, far outranking demographic or enrollment details. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on the first try, your metrics are on the lower end, especially those deciles and LSAT, which often signal challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can realistically improve: retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above the average, build stronger study habits to lift your grades in any remaining coursework, and seek targeted prep like bar exam tutoring or academic coaching early on. Many students in similar positions have turned things around by addressing these gaps head-on. You're already showing determination by applying—keep building on that, and consider reapplying after shoring up these key areas. We believe in second chances and wish you the best in strengthening your path forward.
